did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and eight pre-trained deep learning models (ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet) to classify three types of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, and Benign Keratosis Lesion) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and evaluation of eight pre-trained deep learning models (ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet) for skin lesion classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific methodology, training/testing phases, and quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesions Recognition System Using Various Pre-trained Models
journal: 2021 1st International Conference on Emerging Smart Technologies and Applications (eSmarTA)
brief_summary: This study developed an automated skin lesion recognition system using convolutional neural networks to classify three types of lesions: Melanoma, Nevus, and Benign Keratosis Lesion. The researchers implemented and evaluated eight pre-trained deep learning models, including ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet. The experimental dataset consisted of approximately 3,000 dermoscopic images sourced from the ISIC 2019 Challenge, with 1,000 images per class. The models were assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics across training and testing phases. The results indicated that InceptionResNetV2 achieved the highest performance, reaching an accuracy of 0.875 in the training phase and 0.87 in the testing phase. The study concludes that deep learning models can effectively assist in the early detection and classification of skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes used in the classification task. Approximately 1000 images were included from the ISIC 2019 dataset. Identified as melanocyte-borne cancer.
One of the three primary classes used in the classification task. Approximately 1000 images were included from the ISIC 2019 dataset. Also referred to as nevi or moles.

One of the three primary classes used in the classification task. Approximately 1000 images were included from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The paper notes that BKL includes seborrheic keratosis, solar lentigo, and lichen planus-like keratosis.

all_task_types.target: Three discrete skin lesion classes: Melanoma, Nevus, and Benign Keratosis Lesion (BKL)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a multi-class classification task to distinguish between three types of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, and Benign Keratosis Lesion) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories, with performance evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The best model (InceptionResNetV2) achieved an accuracy of 0.8733 in the testing phase.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, specifically from the 2019 Challenge. The paper notes that the full ISIC dataset includes more than 25,200 images related to 8 skin lesion classes, but this study utilized a specific subset of approximately 3,000 dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the eight pre-trained deep learning models (ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet).
use.validation: Used for validation (loss calculated and reported in figures).
use.testing: Used for testing the models to evaluate performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: InceptionResNetV2 achieved the best results in terms of accuracy, precision, and F1-score. VGG-11 had the worst recall and F1-score. The abstract also mentions InceptionResNetV2 reached 0.875 in the training phase and 0.87 in the testing phase, while the conclusion states testing accuracy of 0.8733 for InceptionResNetV2 and 0.8566 for GoogLeNet. Table I provides the detailed metrics for all models.

architecture_used.main_model: Eight pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (1000 classes). Weights were loaded onto the convolutional basis of the models.

architecture_details.ResNet: Uses skip ties/shorts to leap over several steps. Double or third-layer skips with intermediate nonlinearity (ReLU) and batch normalization.
architecture_details.AlexNet: ANN used in the implementation of the first layer of pre-trained models. Won ILSVRC-2012.
architecture_details.DenseNet121: Uses dense connections where feature maps for each subsequent layer are used as inputs. Mitigates vanishing gradient, improves feature distribution, promotes reusability of functions, and minimizes parameters.
architecture_details.GoogLeNet: 22 layers deep. Uses 1x1 convolutions for dimension reduction, global average pooling (reducing trainable parameters to 0 in that layer), and ReLU activation. Dropout ratio of 0.7 used for regularization.
architecture_details.InceptionResNetV2: Based on Inception architectural family with residual connections (replacing filter concatenation stage of Inception).

training_details.method: Transfer learning with fine-tuning. The last four layers of the pre-trained models were removed and replaced with new layers adapted to the 3-class dataset.
training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy (implied by softmax classifier in original models) and custom loss minimized during training. Specific loss values reported: GoogLeNet training loss reached 0.02; validation loss ranged from 0.45 (VGG11) to 0.32 (ResNet101).

hyperparameters.learning_rate: Not reported. Paper notes 'hyperparameter tuning and learning rate schedules can be done for DenseNets' as future work.

training_details.training_schedule: Not explicitly reported. Epochs mentioned: GoogLeNet validation loss reached best performance at epoch 8.

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom fully connected layers replacing the original 1000-class softmax classifier.
classification_or_output_head.details: The original classification layer (1000 classes) was removed. New layers were installed to classify the 3 specific skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Benign Keratosis Lesion).

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported. Paper notes GoogLeNet has 'considerably lower' parameters than previous winners and DenseNets need 'far fewer parameters' than traditional L-layered CNNs.

conclusions: Deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), can effectively assist in the early detection and classification of skin lesions.
Among the eight pre-trained models evaluated (ResNet18, ResNet-50, ResNet101, VGG11, DenseNet121, InceptionResNetV2, AlexNet, and GoogLeNet), InceptionResNetV2 achieved the highest performance.
InceptionResNetV2 achieved an accuracy of 0.875 in the training phase and 0.8733 (reported as 0.87 in the abstract) in the testing phase.
VGG11 demonstrated the worst performance among the tested models, with an accuracy of 0.8266, precision of 0.8362, recall of 0.8268, and F1-score of 0.8277.
Transfer learning using pre-trained models is a viable approach for skin lesion classification, mitigating the need for large datasets and high processing capacity required for training from scratch.
The study acknowledges that further accuracy improvements can be made through comprehensive hyperparameter tuning and learning rate schedules, particularly for DenseNets.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small, balanced dataset of only 3,000 images (1,000 per class) from the ISIC 2019 Challenge, which may not reflect the real-world prevalence or complexity of skin lesions.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the provided ISIC 2019 test split, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the differences between models.

Data leakage risks are present as the paper does not explicitly detail the train/validation/test split methodology or confirm that no patient overlap exists between splits, which is critical for ISIC datasets.

The study acknowledges that further accuracy improvements are possible through hyperparameter tuning and learning rate schedules, suggesting the current results may not represent the optimal performance of the models.

The dataset includes Benign Keratosis Lesions (BKL) which aggregate multiple distinct pathological entities (seborrheic keratosis, solar lentigo, lichen planus-like keratosis), potentially introducing intra-class heterogeneity.
The models were implemented using transfer learning with pre-trained weights from ImageNet, which may introduce bias towards natural images rather than medical dermoscopic images.

The paper does not report computational complexity, inference time, or hardware specifications beyond the software environment (Google Colab/Matlab), limiting assessment of clinical deployment feasibility.
